_meta.notes: Ch2 L5. Lola, lolo, mano po.

cards.body: Remember 'your grandmother' from Chapter 1? Her real name is Lola. And grandpa is Lolo.
Lola means 'grandmother.' The heart of every Filipino household.

example.note: Opo, not oo. Lolo gets the respectful yes.

cards.source_text: Lola asks: 'Anak, kumain ka na ba?'
cards.prompt: How do you say yes respectfully?
cards.explanation: Opo = respectful yes. Lola always gets po/opo.

cards.body: Mano po is a gesture of respect. You take an elder's hand and press the back of it to your forehead.

example.note: Done when greeting elders. Usually Lola, Lolo, Tita, Tito.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Mano' comes from Spanish 'mano' (hand). The gesture itself predates the Spanish, but the word stuck.

cards.source_text: You arrive at Lolo's house for dinner.
options: Say 'Mano po, Lolo!' and take his hand
cards.explanation: Mano po with elders when you arrive. It shows respect and love.

cards.source_text: Your grandmother greets you at the door.
cards.explanation: Mano po, Lola! The respectful greeting gesture.

cards.prompt: Greet your grandmother respectfully and ask how she is.
cards.explanation: Mano po, Lola! Kumusta po kayo? Full respectful greeting with formal 'you.'

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: Mano po isn't just for grandparents. Any elder relative gets it: tita, tito, ninong, ninang. Some families even do it with parents.
In the Philippines, Lola is often the real boss of the household. Never refuse her food.
Lolo and Lola are also used for elderly strangers as a sign of affection and respect.
